author: @thepvporg
text: Change print build orientation to avoid using supports.

author: @stano1603
text: Hi Rob, Try using vaporized acetone. I use to work in the Packaging Industry and when we would design up a new jar or bottle we would get a clear 3D printed model made of the jar or bottle for evaluation and marketing purposes.  To enhance the optics of the 3D printed model the container would be hand sanded to remove many of the ridges produced as part of the 3D printing process.  After the sanding and compressed air cleaning the model would be placed in an enclosure and acetone via a vent port would be slightly heated so as to vaporize and that vapor would be circulated inside the enclosure.  This then resulted in effectively just slightly melting the surface of the model producing a relatively smooth and clearer finish.  As an alternative to consider, last year I bought one of those kits on Amazon to repair the outer finished surface on the headlights of my car.  Yes my car is over 10 years old.  The vapor deposition kit available on Amazon that looks something similar to a heated thermos coffee cup with a funnel on the top worked great at restoring the finish of the plastic lens on the lights on my car.  Always have good air flow when doing either of these processes and no open flames or sparks in the vicinity.  Regards.

author: @icebluscorpion
text: Hey Rob, here's another trick/tip get some UV resistant clear blue diy for UV resin add 3 to 4 drops in the resin mix it and print with that mixture to prevent yellowing of the resin. It has to be clear blue non suspensionous diy to get near optical great resin. Otherwise if the diy is not dissolving in resin you have like tiny particles that give unwanted interference with the light

author: @ashtang2029
text:
Hello Sir,

We are a group of students participating in an Engineering project, our project is mainly generating energy from a Tribo-electric nanogenerator, we had seen  your video on TNT omnibus channel beforehand, we'd like your advice and help on making the triboelectric nanogenerator and how we'd connect multiple cells to maximise power production, we have many questions as well, like whether we'd be able to store this charge in a capacitor or a lithium ion battery and use it later on ? We have planned to make the TENG out of PTFE (plumber's tape) and Aluminium foil, moreover we weren't able to access the article mentioned in your video, any help would be instrumental and highly helpful
thank you

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate - I am afraid  get something like 3-400 emails, posts and comments every day and in them will be 20-30 requests just like yours. I am sure you can see it is just not possible for me to respond to all of them. As a consequence the only real help I can offer is contained in the videos I make. I do wish you the best with your project but it is not possible for me to help on an individual basis - kind regards - Rob

author: @richardglickman7203
text:
Way back, in the 60’s & 70’s, when I processed my film and made my own prints. If a negative got scratched we would apply a small amount of petroleum jelly to the negative and then buff it with cotton balls. The petroleum jelly filled in the scratch preventing the scratch from refracting light when enlarging the image. 

Jewelers use some very fine buffing compounds to polish some very soft metals. If you polish the lens so all the scratches are parallel the resin may work better at filling in the scratches and reducing refraction to produce a clearer lens.

You may even produce a polarized lens.

author: @dav1dbone
text:
I've probably every Dremel attachment, I've never had success in plastic polishing, yet take a piece of equipment that has a plastic facia etc, only a little bit of use and over time granted - that amount of shine you get? 
Something worth trying if you are working frequently with a resin/filament is to see how it reacts with various solvents, isopropyl alcohol, ethyl acetate, acetone, MEK, brake cleaner and the "beast" - xylene, obviously there are others, but just getting a feel of the material, to me is key, for example, printing in abs and you are struggling with bed adhesion? Try rubbing a spare/scrap raft over some MEK, it really helps. These new resins that print almost clear, it may be worth experimenting with solvents to see if opacity can be improved 😅

author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
text:
Sir.. do you have a solution for perhaps being able to twist an image, radially.
As in we use yaw , pitch and roll.
Then I wanna roll the image to an extent, through the use of the lense.
Like make incoming or outgoing image from portrait to landscape and vice versa.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
An 'erector lens' as used in telescopes etc can do this, as you rotate it, it rotates the image.
I think its sometimes called a 'dove' prism.

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
replies.text:
@bikerfirefarter7280  ahh yes!
I'd heard about it.. forgot the term though.
And I have no idea how it is supposed to work. Never seen a visual illustration of the thing.
I'd only heard the name dove prism though.
Not the erector lens.
Also, had no idea it could vary the degree of rotation too!
Very awesome!!!
Much thanks!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think bikerfirefarter gave a really good reply answer mate

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering  I'd be having a really hard time contemplating how it works though.
As, I can make one (theoretical design) myself even.. probably.. but it likely wont be as precise or useful.
As he mentioned, the dove prism is able to twist the image variably too.
And no design I've yet come up with for a lens.. that can do that.
Soo.. I'm not able to understand how it works. ); . I'll try though.
You're one hardworking man.
And I intend to.. do my own research, and try and find out.
Thanks you.
